title: Leverage Multipliers

content.statement: Each action should multiply, not add.
content.question: Where are you adding effort when you should add leverage?
sop: List your five biggest time sinks from the past two weeks.
For each, identify a leverage point available to you: team, capital, systems, or brand/audience.
Before doing the task yourself again, ask which leverage point could absorb it instead.
Estimate your Output (dollars or units produced) against Time spent (hours) for your biggest task this week (see calculation, page 4), and target whichever ratio is worst.
Replace at least one recurring task with a leveraged version (delegated, automated, templated) this week.
questions: Where are you adding effort when you should add leverage?
What did you personally do this week that a tool, template, or person could have done instead?
If you stopped working for a month, which parts of your output would keep happening anyway?
content.equation_example: Output (units produced or dollars generated) divided by Your Time (hours) equals Leverage ratio. Output $10,000 divided by Your Time 20 hours equals 500. Higher/rising means each hour of your time produces more; lower/falling means you're trading hours for results at a flat, unscalable rate.
content.closing_statement: Multiply your time before you spend more of it.

caption:
Principle Nº 171: Leverage Multipliers

Each action should multiply, not add.

Ask yourself: Where are you adding effort when you should add leverage?
